description: A step-by-step dialogue example showing the Self-Ask prompting method, where the LLM decomposes a complex question into follow-up sub-questions, issues external search queries for each, retrieves answers, and synthesizes a final answer.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how Self-Ask breaks multi-hop reasoning into explicit intermediate steps with external search, helping students understand how LLMs can be guided to decompose problems rather than answer in one shot.
when_to_show: Show when introducing retrieval-augmented or decomposition-based prompting strategies, especially to contrast with standard single-step prompting.
suggested_caption: Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Press et al. 2022)

description: A diagram comparing four reasoning strategies: Input→Output (IO), Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Self-Consistency with CoT, and Tree of Thoughts (ToT), showing how ToT explores multiple reasoning branches as a tree structure with evaluation at each node.
teaching_value: Visually contrasts the progression from simple IO prompting to tree-structured exploration, helping students understand why ToT can outperform linear CoT by allowing backtracking and evaluating multiple reasoning paths.
when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced reasoning prompting methods, particularly when comparing CoT variants and explaining how tree-search improves complex problem solving.
suggested_caption: Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths beyond linear CoT. (Yao et al. 2022)

description: A side-by-side comparison of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program of Thoughts (PoT) prompting on a math word problem, showing CoT producing natural language reasoning steps while PoT generates Python code that is executed by an interpreter to compute the answer.
teaching_value: Clearly demonstrates the key difference between CoT (reasoning in natural language) and PoT (offloading computation to code execution), helping students understand when and why using a programming language as an intermediate representation is advantageous.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how LLMs can use code as a reasoning medium, especially for arithmetic or symbolic tasks where natural language reasoning is error-prone.
suggested_caption: PoT offloads computation to Python code, unlike CoT's natural language steps. (Chen et al. 2022)

description: A formatted text example showing the TALM API call structure, with special tokens |tool-call, tool input, |result, and |output demarcating how the LM generates an API request, receives a result, and produces a final output within a single text sequence.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract concept of tool-augmented LMs concrete by showing the exact token-level format used to embed API calls within text generation, helping students understand how tool use is implemented as a text-to-text process.
when_to_show: Show when introducing tool-augmented language models and explaining how API calls are structured within the LM's text generation pipeline.
suggested_caption: TALM uses special tokens to embed API calls within text generation. (Parisi et al. 2022)

description: A line graph showing model performance (accuracy or similar metric) across multiple self-play iterations in TALM, demonstrating that iterative bootstrapping of tool-use examples progressively improves the model's performance.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that the self-play iterative training loop in TALM is effective, helping students understand why iterative dataset expansion with tool feedback leads to measurable gains.
when_to_show: Show when discussing the training methodology of TALM and the value of self-play loops for improving tool-use capabilities in LLMs.
suggested_caption: TALM self-play iterations progressively improve tool-use performance. (Parisi et al. 2022)

description: A pipeline diagram illustrating how Toolformer is built: starting from raw text, sampling candidate API call positions, executing API calls, filtering by whether results reduce language modeling loss, and fine-tuning the LM on the resulting annotated dataset.
teaching_value: Shows the full self-supervised pipeline for teaching an LLM to use tools without heavy human annotation, helping students understand how Toolformer bootstraps tool-use ability from a small number of demonstrations.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how Toolformer differs from TALM and how self-supervised filtering of API calls enables scalable tool-use training.
suggested_caption: Toolformer's pipeline: sample, execute, filter, and fine-tune on API calls. (Schick et al. 2023)

description: An annotated text example showing how Toolformer inserts API call annotations into a sentence, with calculator, Wikipedia search, and translation tool calls marked inline, demonstrating the format used to create the self-supervised training dataset.
teaching_value: Concretely shows students what the annotated training data looks like for Toolformer, making it clear how diverse tools (calculator, search, translation) are uniformly represented as inline API calls within natural text.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the data annotation step of Toolformer training, particularly to illustrate how multiple tool types are handled with a unified annotation format.
suggested_caption: Toolformer annotates text with inline API calls for self-supervised training. (Schick et al. 2023)

description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).
teaching_value: Gives students a mental map of the entire agent system before diving into individual components, helping them understand how planning, memory, and tool use interconnect around the LLM core.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall system structure before explaining each subsystem in detail.
suggested_caption: LLM agent architecture: planning, memory, and tool use components. (Weng, 2023)

description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how ReAct interleaves reasoning traces with actions, making the abstract concept of 'reasoning + acting' tangible through real task examples and showing why the Thought step matters.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the ReAct framework, specifically to contrast the Thought-Action-Observation loop against an Act-only approach and explain why reasoning traces improve performance.
suggested_caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories across knowledge and decision-making tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)

description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle between Actor (LLM), Evaluator (reward/heuristic), Self-Reflection module, and Memory buffer, with arrows indicating how failed trajectories feed into self-reflection and updated memory for the next trial.
teaching_value: Visualizes the feedback loop that enables iterative self-improvement in agents, clarifying how dynamic memory and self-reflection work together to correct mistakes across trials.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how agents can self-correct, particularly after introducing the concept of trial-and-error in autonomous agents and before discussing the heuristic function details.
suggested_caption: Reflexion's self-reflection loop: actor, evaluator, and memory buffer. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)

description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across multiple trials.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for Reflexion's effectiveness and quantifies the relative frequency of hallucination versus inefficient planning as failure modes, grounding theoretical concepts in measurable outcomes.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to validate its claims with data, and use the hallucination vs. inefficient planning breakdown to deepen discussion of agent failure modes.
suggested_caption: Reflexion outperforms baselines; hallucination dominates over inefficient planning. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)

description: A diagram showing how Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning works: a sequence of model outputs annotated with feedback labels (Bad, Good, Better, etc.) is fed as a prefix, and the model learns to produce incrementally improved outputs conditioned on that sequence.
teaching_value: Makes the CoH training mechanism concrete by showing how ranked feedback sequences are structured as input, helping students understand how the model learns to self-improve from its own history.
when_to_show: Show when teaching how models can learn from ranked feedback sequences, especially as a contrast to RLHF, to illustrate an alternative self-reflection training approach.
suggested_caption: CoH fine-tuning: model improves outputs conditioned on ranked feedback sequences. (Liu et al., 2023)

description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD): source policies generate multi-episode learning histories for various tasks, which are then used to train a neural network via behavioral cloning to produce a task-agnostic in-context RL policy.
teaching_value: Clarifies the AD training pipeline — from source policy history generation to behavioral cloning — helping students understand how in-context reinforcement learning can be distilled into a single model.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Algorithm Distillation as a method for in-context RL, particularly to explain how learning histories are structured and used for training.
suggested_caption: Algorithm Distillation: distilling RL learning histories into a neural network. (Laskin et al., 2023)

description: Performance comparison charts of AD, ED (Expert Distillation), source policy, and RL² across memory and exploration environments (dark environments with A3C, watermaze with DQN), measuring cumulative reward over episodes with binary reward.
teaching_value: Empirically demonstrates AD's advantage over baselines in environments requiring memory and exploration, and shows the importance of multi-episode context for achieving near-optimal in-context RL.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the AD mechanism to provide empirical validation, and use to discuss why multi-episode context length matters for in-context RL emergence.
suggested_caption: AD outperforms ED and RL² on memory/exploration tasks with binary reward. (Laskin et al., 2023)

description: A hierarchical diagram categorizing human memory into sensory memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), short-term/working memory, and long-term memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), with mappings to LLM agent memory equivalents.
teaching_value: Bridges cognitive science and AI by mapping human memory types to LLM agent components (e.g., in-context storage, external vector databases), helping students understand the design rationale for agent memory systems.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the memory module to establish the cognitive science analogy before explaining how each memory type is implemented in LLM agent systems.
suggested_caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)

description: A comparison chart of Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) / Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) measured by recall@10 versus query speed, showing the accuracy-speed tradeoff.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the practical tradeoffs when choosing a vector search algorithm for agent memory retrieval, making the abstract concept of ANN concrete with measurable performance metrics.
when_to_show: Show when discussing external memory implementation and vector databases, specifically to explain why approximate (not exact) search is used and how to choose among ANN algorithms.
suggested_caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed tradeoffs. (Google Blog, 2020)

description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user input flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how an LLM can orchestrate multiple specialized models as tools, making abstract agent architecture tangible by showing task decomposition, dependency tracking, and result aggregation in a single visual flow.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, specifically to demonstrate how a real system (HuggingGPT) chains task planning → model selection → execution → response into a coherent pipeline.
suggested_caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, executes, and responds. (Shen et al. 2023)

description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, and result evaluation across multiple decision levels.
teaching_value: Makes explicit the multi-step reasoning an LLM must perform for tool use — not just calling an API, but deciding whether to call one, which to call, and how to verify the result — helping students understand evaluation granularity in agent benchmarks.
when_to_show: Show when discussing how to evaluate an agent's tool use capability, particularly to contrast the three levels of API-Bank assessment and highlight where LLM reasoning can fail in a tool-augmented pipeline.
suggested_caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM API-call decision logic. (Li et al. 2023)

description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations, stores them in a memory stream, runs reflection to form higher-level insights, and uses planning to drive behavior in a simulated social environment.
teaching_value: Illustrates how memory, reflection, and planning modules combine to produce believable long-term agent behavior, giving students a concrete reference architecture for understanding how agents maintain context and simulate human-like cognition over time.
when_to_show: Show when teaching memory and planning components of LLM agents, especially to demonstrate how reflection bridges raw observations and high-level planning in a multi-agent social simulation context.
suggested_caption: Generative agent architecture: memory, reflection, and planning modules. (Park et al. 2023)
